statement: Support for EU membership in Iceland is strongly correlated with economic distress. During the 2008–2010 financial crisis, when GDP contracted by 10.5% cumulatively and the króna lost 50% of its value, support for EU membership surged from ~35% to ~55%. As the economy recovered (2011–2016), support fell back to 25–30%. A secondary uptick to ~40% occurred during the 2022–2023 inflation episode (CPI 8.3–8.7%). This pattern suggests that EU/euro membership is viewed partly as an insurance policy against economic instability rather than a principled commitment to European integration. The correlation coefficient between consumer confidence and EU support is approximately -0.6 (negative: lower confidence = higher EU support).
source_name: Gallup Ísland — EU polls cross-referenced with Hagstofa consumer confidence index
caveats: Correlation is not causation — other factors (government composition, media coverage, international events) also influence EU support. The current referendum occurs during a period of moderate economic performance, which may reduce crisis-driven support. If the economy weakens before August 2026, the 'Yes' side could benefit.

related_entries.statement: Icelandic public opinion on EU membership has swung dramatically since the 2009 application. Support peaked at approximately 55% in early 2009, during the financial crisis when EU/euro membership was seen as a stability anchor. By 2013, support had fallen to approximately 25% as the economy recovered and the centre-right government suspended negotiations. Support gradually recovered to ~30% by 2018, ~35% by 2022, and ~45% by late 2025. The 2024 Althingi vote to hold a binding referendum re-energised the debate. Throughout this period, the proportion of undecided voters ranged from 15–25%, representing the decisive swing group.

The Icelandic króna has experienced three major crisis episodes since 2000. During the 2008 banking collapse, the króna lost approximately 50% of its value against the euro (from ~90 ISK/EUR in mid-2007 to ~185 ISK/EUR by early 2009), necessitating capital controls that lasted from 2008 to 2017. A second episode of sharp depreciation occurred in early 2020 at the onset of COVID-19, with the króna weakening from ~138 to ~162 ISK/EUR (~17% depreciation). A third period of weakness occurred in 2023–2024, with the króna trading above 150 ISK/EUR. These episodes illustrate the vulnerability of a small independent currency, but critics note that króna flexibility also aided recovery — Iceland's post-2008 rebound was faster than that of peripheral eurozone members who could not devalue.

citations.report_title: 25. þáttur Silfursins: Stefnt að kosningu um ESB í sumar

claims.claim_text: Í yfir tíu ár hefur þjóðin verið klofin um ESB-málið eftir að kosturinn var tekinn af borðinu.
Ríkisstjórnin setti ESB-málið á oddinn vegna þess að hún var komin í þrot með efnahagsmálin sem hún var kosin út á — verðbólgu, vexti og stöðu heimilanna.
Í tíð vinstristjórnarinnar var markmiðið að nota tækifærið í hruninu til að koma Íslandi inn í ESB, og mátti helst ekkert ganga upp á forsendum Íslands af því að það mátti ekki styggja ESB.

citations.report_title: Að kljúfa þjóðina með tilfinningaþrunginni spurningu

claims.claim_text: Kristrún Frostadóttir sagði í ágúst 2024 að þetta væri ekki rétti tíminn til að fara í aðildarviðræður við ESB.

citations.report_title: Engin fordæmi fyrir leið Íslands í átt að ESB

claims.claim_text: Efnahagslegar og hernaðarlegar breytingar í heiminum hafa breytt forsendum ESB-umræðunnar.

citations.report_title: Ræða þarf alla kaflana á nýjan leik

claims.claim_text: Margir sem studdu ESB-aðild vildu fá evru í stað íslensku krónunnar.
Eitt af markmiðum ESB-aðildar var að styrkja viðskipti og efnahag Íslands eftir bankahrunið 2008.
